utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: next patient is emily hernandez . date of birth , march 26 2001 . this is a 20 year old female being seen today for our yearly follow-up for asthma , allergic rhinitis and food allergy . due to insurance changes since our last office appointment in april 2020 , she was switched from symbicort to wixela . she also uses zyrtec in the spring , summer and fall for her seasonal allergy symptoms , which tend to work well . she also has known peanut allergy , she does tolerate almonds and cashews . please put in the asthma section at the very bottom that the act score is 21 . please put under interval history , written consent is obtained today to use dax for documentation .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hi emily , how are you ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm pretty good , how are you ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm good , thank you . so gosh , we last talked a little bit over a year ago and it was just a telemedicine visit , but it seems like you've been doing pretty good . i know there was an insurance change . i think you had switched over to wixela , is that right ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yep , that's right , and it's what i'm currently using .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , and that's the 250/50 dose , correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , correct .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: perfect . so how does that compare ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean , it's working well . i really have n't had any issues with it and i take it once in the morning and once at night .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and that's one puff twice a day ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and have you been having to use your albuterol inhaler at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: rarely , and if i do it's really just before a workout or something out that , if i know i'm really going to be pushing it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so it sounds like it's around strenuous activity .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so you can do things like go on a walk or going to the grocery store no problem , and just use it for more exertional stuff ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . like , if i'm gon na go for a run or something like that i'll really just take it before as a preventative .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and i've been trying to use my daily if i can a little less because i'm trying to go into the military soon .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: no kidding , that's great .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , so i have to be able to operate basically at a certain fitness level without an inhaler .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: really ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , and if i can prove that i'm capable of these activities without an inhaler it makes it easier .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and so by inhaler do they mean both your daily and rescue inhalers ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm . so it's like they'll have a fitness test and basically they'll tell me not to take my inhaler that day before the test , and then they'll see how the asthma affects me . and they're looking to see if it's serious enough to hold me back from joining or whatever . so i have been taking it less and less . and really some days i feel like i do n't need it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and that is the wixela that you're not taking . so on average per week , how many days of the week do you use one puff in the morning and one puff at night ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: probably about three or four .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , three to four days per week and the other days it's once a day ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , that's correct .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and it's either in the morning or at night that i'm taking it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , and do you think that's working okay ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: great . and any urgent care or er visits at all for breathing over the past year ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nope .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , so i'm going to assume no one has put you on prednisone for breathing issues then .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nope .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and now , military wise , what's the time frame of when you'll be completing some of these exertional tests ? like , what are your next steps ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so , hopefully i'm going to be enlisted early spring of 2022 . so that gives me about six to seven months to get to a point where i'm able to exert my body where i do n't really need the inhaler . or at least show them that i have it under control .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: now when you were in the other room you completed a breathing test , correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: correct .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , great . so now let me ask you about the allergies , 'cause i know typically you'll use the zyrtec in the spring , summer and fall , correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , i do n't really ... um , or i'm sorry , i use it more during the spring and fall . i do n't really have to use it too much in the summer .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: do you use it more just as needed in the summer ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , that's right .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and have you been taking the zyrtec every day recently since you're right in the mix of spring ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not every day , but i do take it on days i know i'm going to be outside a lot . um , i do tend to work outside about three days a week so i'll definitely take it on those days .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and does it seem to work pretty well still ? are you happy with it ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , it does , it works great .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wonderful , good to hear . and then i'm assuming you're staying away from the peanuts . uh , last year you had told me it worked with ... uh , you worked with enterprise rent-a-car and one of the cars had a lot of peanuts in it that you were cleaning out and that gave you hives , uh , when you had touched it , is that correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yep , that's correct .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and has there been anything like that since that experience ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , i do n't think so . i definitely became more cautious after that , so i usually wear , uh , wear long sleeves and change my gloves every time now . so i have n't had a reaction , but i imagine that i probably came into contact with cars that had peanuts in them , but they just d- did n't touch my skin .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: super . and it sounds like you're really smart about it .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , and the airplanes , they have peanuts . they give everyone too , so .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and you have an epipen that k- that you keep with you , correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and i'm assuming you're going to need refills , um , i can call you in a new set for the year . typically of course , they expire within a year .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and i remember , um , sometime previously you've had some testing for tree nuts , but you do have almonds and cashews , correct ? and- and you do okay with them , or do you avoid all tree nuts ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do tend to avoid all of them but i recall eating some almonds at one point , uh , really soon after we discussed i might not be allergic to tree nuts anymore .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: and i remember having them and nothing happened , but i usually just tend to stay away from them , um , really if i can help it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so sometimes when people have a peanut allergy , the nuts in general just are n't very important to them , so it is very common to avoid both . um , so let's do this . i'll have helen come in and i would love to complete a breathing test on you . if your breathing numbers are looking great we can work on getting you , uh , something lower than wixela . if you look at all of our asthma regimens it is a stepwise process , and wixela is a combination of two medications in one . and , well maybe you do n't need both of'em . uh , maybe you just need one of'em . so we can work our way backwards .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and so the breathing test will help give us some- some more guidance , we will look at the results in just a moment .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right , and so this past year i did get into the bad habit of vaping and smoking , but i have been trying to cut that out . so i should be good , and , um , now i only do just a little bit and i am trying to quit . so i am sure that might have an effect .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it can . um , so what we can do today is compare your results with prior results , probably before you started smoking or using the vape .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah . and we'll see where you stand at that point .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right , that sounds good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: well let me go ahead and listen , so hop on up here for me .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right so your ears look good , a little bit of wax on both sides . can i see inside your mouth ? big ahh .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: ahh .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good . okay let me take a peek in your nose . all right , good . you can put your mask back on .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and go ahead take a big breath in and out . good , your lungs sound terrific . i'll listen to your heart , breathe quietly . okay very good .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: awesome .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so your breathing test is about the same as it was a couple years ago , and actually looks a little bit better . you may not remember but when we last completed the test in 2019 , uh , do you recall if you were feeling lousy at the time ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think it was the smoke . there were so many fires at that point and i remember coming in and looking out the window and all you saw was the orange and the smoke .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yes , you're absolutely right , that was a bad year . that was my first fall here and i was really alarmed . well that explains why at that time your numbers were a little bit lower and today they look better . here's what i think , so you'll have kaiser currently , correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: correct .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . most insurances do limit what we can use .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i do know that .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so option one would be to continue using wixela like you're using it now . we would classify it as off-label and you can use it once a day instead of twice a day , which is fine . some people do that because they just need it once a day , so that's an option . option two would be to actually back up to an inhaler that just has one med .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so the one that kaiser covers is alvesco . the thing about alvesco , though , is that it would be two puffs in the morning and two puffs at night . so it's a bit more work . um , it's not breath activated but we could step down the lowest dose to see how you do . some of the newer guidelines for asthma that were published this year is recommending trying to treat people with inhalers like wixela or alvesco only when you're ill , or say during smoke season . so minimizing their use to those periods of time versus all year . unless you found out that you're getting [ inaudible 00:09:25 ] sick having issues every month , then that would indicate would pull back too much .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that we had too much , yeah .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: um , so we could step back and possibly get you to the point where you just use those inhalers , uh , when you're ill or it's smoke season , but at the first step to do any of that would be to try to drop down to just alvesco .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , that actually sounds really good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay great . so i will call that in for you , it will be the lower dose , 80 mcg , and you'll have to do two puffs twice a day .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and that'll be on the box when i call it in . you can put the wixela off to the side , but do n't throw it away just in case the alvesco does n't work well for you .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , got it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and then ideally we would repeat your breathing test in four months to see how you're doing , and then be able to document that you're doing well . sometimes , not always , the military will require documentation on breathing tests .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: is your pharmacy still the same or did you update it with helen ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i believe she updated it . it should be kaiser permanente broadway .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay perfect . i'll make sure it's the alvesco and then i'll renew your albuterol and epipen as well .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: awesome , thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're welcome . uh , then the zyrtec , do you pick it up over the counter or do get- do you get it as a prescription ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i just get it over the counter .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay got it . and do you have a nebulizer that you would , uh , need refills on ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't think i need any refills , i have n't used that in such a long time . i think the last time was when i had bronchitis , or there was few select times when i had to use it when i was kid .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay good . well i think we have our game plan .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay awesome .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and do the alvesco two puffs twice a day and your albuterol if you need it . the zyrtec 10 milligrams as you need it and continue to stay away from peanuts and tree nuts and i'll refill your epipen .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right so we've got it .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: great .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right i'll have you take this to the front and i'm gon na say four months re-check for asthma and we'll repeat the spirometer- spirometry at that time .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay sounds good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right well thanks so much and you have a great day .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you too .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and we'll do a follow-up in four months with act and spirometry , sooner if needed . follow-up 25 minutes .

detail_list.summary: Emily Hernandez is a 20-year-old female presenting for annual follow-up for asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergy.
The patient switched from Symbicort to Wixela due to insurance changes in April 2020.
The patient uses Zyrtec for seasonal allergies and has known peanut allergies while tolerating almonds and cashews.
Asthma
The doctor asks if the patient is using the 250/50 dose of Wixela.
The patient takes Wixela 250/50 one puff twice daily.
The doctor asks if the patient uses a rescue albuterol inhaler.
The patient uses albuterol rarely, primarily before strenuous exercise.
The patient is attempting to reduce inhaler use to meet military fitness requirements.
The doctor asks for the weekly frequency of Wixela use.
The patient uses Wixela twice daily for 3-4 days per week, and once daily on other days.
The doctor asks about urgent care or ER visits for breathing in the past year.
The patient denies urgent care or ER visits for breathing in the past year.
The doctor asks if the patient has required prednisone for breathing issues.
The patient denies requiring prednisone for breathing issues in the past year.
The doctor asks about the timeline for military exertional testing.
The patient aims to enlist in the military in early spring 2022 and has 6-7 months to demonstrate fitness without inhalers.
Allergies
The patient uses Zyrtec primarily in the spring and fall, and as needed in the summer.
The patient takes Zyrtec 3 days per week when working outside.
The patient had a previous contact hive reaction to peanuts while cleaning a rental car.
The patient avoids peanut exposure by wearing long sleeves and changing gloves when cleaning cars.
The doctor asks if the patient carries an EpiPen.
The patient confirms she carries an EpiPen.
The doctor asks about the patient's tree nut allergy status.
The patient avoids all tree nuts but tolerated almonds previously.
The patient admits to vaping and smoking but is attempting to quit.
Physical exam of ears, mouth, and nose performed; findings were normal.
Lung and heart exams were normal.
Current spirometry results are better than 2019 results.
The patient attributes poor 2019 spirometry results to wildfire smoke.
The doctor prescribes Alvesco 80 mcg, two puffs twice daily.
The patient will return in four months for repeat ACT and spirometry testing.
The doctor will renew the patient's albuterol and EpiPen prescriptions.
The doctor asks if the patient needs a nebulizer refill.
The patient does not need a nebulizer refill.

case_description: follow up for asthma; allergic rhinitis; food allergy; none
